title: Timing Concepts
summary: Setup, hold, clock skew, and metastability — making circuits work at speed.

sections.heading: Synchronous Design
sections.body: Most digital ASICs and FPGAs use synchronous design: state changes occur on clock edges. Combinational logic between registers must settle before the next edge. The clock period must exceed the longest path delay plus setup time.
bullets: Launch edge → combinational delay → capture edge
Single clock domain simplifies timing analysis

sections.heading: Setup and Hold Time
sections.body: Setup time (tsu): data must be stable BEFORE the capturing clock edge. Hold time (th): data must remain stable AFTER the edge. Violating either causes unpredictable flip-flop behavior.
bullets: Setup violation: fix by slowing clock or shortening logic path
Hold violation: fix by adding delay on data path (buffer)
Static timing analysis (STA) checks all paths automatically

callout.text: Hold violations cannot be fixed by slowing the clock — they need physical delay insertion.

sections.heading: Clock Skew and Jitter
sections.body: Clock skew is the difference in arrival time of the clock at different flip-flops. Positive skew can help setup on short paths; excessive skew hurts timing. Jitter is cycle-to-cycle clock variation.
bullets: Clock tree synthesis balances skew in ASICs
CDC: clock domain crossing requires synchronizers
GCLK buffers distribute FPGA global clocks

sections.heading: Metastability
sections.body: When a flip-flop samples a changing input near the clock edge, it may enter a metastable state — output hovers between 0 and 1 before resolving. MTBF improves exponentially with resolution time (extra synchronizer stages).
bullets: Always use 2+ flip-flop synchronizers for async inputs
MTBF: mean time between metastability failures
Gray code for multi-bit CDC counters

sections.heading: Timing Constraints in Practice
sections.body: SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints) and Xilinx/Intel constraints define clock periods, false paths, and multicycle paths. RTL designers write constraints; STA tools verify before tape-out.

quiz.question: Setup time violation can often be fixed by:
quiz.explanation: Slower clock gives more time for data to arrive before capture edge.

quiz.question: Hold time violation is fixed by:
options: Adding delay on the data path
quiz.explanation: Hold needs data to arrive later — insert buffers/delay cells.

quiz.question: Metastability occurs when:
quiz.explanation: Violating setup/hold around the sampling edge can cause metastability.

quiz.question: Standard CDC synchronizer uses:
quiz.explanation: 2-FF synchronizer gives time for metastable state to resolve.

quiz.question: Clock skew is:
options: Difference in clock arrival at different FFs
quiz.explanation: Skew = spatial variation in clock edge timing across the chip.

flashcards.back: Data stable BEFORE active clock edge (minimum)
Data stable AFTER active clock edge (minimum)
Delay from clock edge to output change

flashcards.front: Why Gray code in CDC counters?
flashcards.back: Only one bit changes per step — safer for synchronizers
Jitter: temporal variation; Skew: spatial arrival difference
